Description of Experience/Practice: Dreams recorded on clay tablets, interpreted as divine messages guiding rulers and society.
Dreams considered prophetic; temples dedicated to dream incubation for healing and guidance.
Ritual use of Soma, a psychoactive substance, for visionary and spiritual experiences.

Phenomenon/Practice: Philosophical dream yoga, states of consciousness
Description of Experience/Practice: Upanishads introduce Brahman/Atman, and describe waking, dreaming, and deep sleep as key states; dreaming seen as a subcomponent of the greater life-dream, with the aim to awaken in all states for moksha (liberation)35.

Phenomenon/Practice: Yogic paths: karma, bhakti, raja, jnana, hatha, kundalini, dream yoga
Description of Experience/Practice: Hinduism develops multiple yogic disciplines; while specific dream yoga is less prominent, the tradition emphasizes awakening from illusion in all states, including dreams3.

Phenomenon/Practice: Waking, dreaming, deep sleep as states of illusion
Description of Experience/Practice: The Mandukya Upanishad analyzes the three states and the 'fourth' (turiya), which transcends them, influencing later dream yoga and nondual practices35.
Techniques to induce conscious awareness in dreams, influencing later Tibetan Dream Yoga.
Aristotle writes about self-awareness in dreams: “something in consciousness declares that what then presents itself is but a dream.”
Used lucid dreams therapeutically, recording cases of healing and guidance through dreams.
Gennadius has a lucid dream with a guide, leading to spiritual transformation and visions of the afterlife.
Use of psychoactive plants (henbane, belladonna) in ointments for visionary experiences.

Phenomenon/Practice: Dream Yoga (Milam, Six Yogas of Naropa)
Description of Experience/Practice: Systematic training in lucid dreaming to attain enlightenment and escape rebirth; dream yoga is a tantric practice passed through Kagyu, Nyingma, Gelug, and Bön lineages. Involves lucid dreaming, fearlessness, realization of emptiness, and dream control124.
Bön tradition has its own dream yoga, focusing on awareness in dreams to recognize the illusory nature of all phenomena4.
Dream yoga is an extension of illusory body yoga; four trainings: retaining awareness, controlling dreams, overcoming fear, meditating on dream suchness1.
Keizan records lucid dream and visionary experiences as part of Zen practice.

Phenomenon/Practice: Lucid dreaming for healing and guidance
Description of Experience/Practice: Lucid dreaming taught from childhood; used for problem-solving, healing, and social harmony.
Described composing and observing comedies in lucid dreams, fully aware of the dream state.

Phenomenon/Practice: Realization of dreaming within a dream
Description of Experience/Practice: Recognized he was dreaming while in a dream, a classic lucid dream experience.
Published a book documenting personal lucid dreams and methods for dream control.

Phenomenon/Practice: Scientific case study of lucid dreams
Description of Experience/Practice: Recorded 352 lucid dreams, coining the term “lucid dream” and analyzing dream types.
Psychedelic culture and music inspired renewed interest in lucid dreaming and altered states.
Published research distinguishing lucid dreams from ordinary dreams, linking them to REM sleep.
First physiological signals sent from within a lucid dream using eye movements, confirming lucidity in the lab.
Used sleep labs and signaling to confirm lucid dreaming as a real, measurable phenomenon; developed induction techniques.
April 12 recognized as a day to celebrate and promote lucid dreaming worldwide.
